title: Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years
subtitle: Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"
caption:
Views about AI's impact on society in the next 20 years

Survey respondents were asked, "Will artificial intelligence help or harm people in the next 20 years?"

This stacked bar chart displays the distribution of public opinion across eleven countries regarding whether artificial intelligence will mostly help, mostly harm, or have a neutral effect on society over the next two decades. The horizontal bars represent the percentage of respondents in each nation, segmented into five categories: "Mostly help" (blue), "Neither" (green), "No response" (purple), "No opinion or don't know" (tan), and "Mostly harm" (maroon). The data comes from a survey of 120,000 to 130,000 people aged 15 and older, with nationally representative samples of approximately 1,000 people per country. Japan and China show the highest levels of optimism, with 61% of respondents in both nations expecting AI to mostly help, while only 13% in each country anticipate it will mostly harm society. Germany follows closely with 60% expecting help and 18% expecting harm. In contrast, Pakistan displays the most pessimistic outlook, where only 19% believe AI will mostly help, and a majority of 52% expect it to mostly harm people. Indonesia also shows low confidence in AI's benefits at 22%, with 33% expecting harm and a large 44% segment expressing no opinion. The United States and India present a divided view; in the US, 35% expect help and 35% expect harm, while in India, 33% expect help and 36% expect harm, with significant portions in both countries selecting "no opinion." European nations like Italy, the United Kingdom, and France show varied distributions, with Italy at 50% expecting help and 32% expecting harm, while France has 40% expecting help and 36% expecting harm. The "Neither" and "No response" categories remain small across most countries, typically under 10%, except for the United Kingdom where "Neither" reaches roughly 5-10%. The dominant pattern shows a wide divergence in global sentiment, ranging from strong optimism in East Asia to predominant skepticism in South Asia.
